dialogue.utterance: Priority one dispatch to Unit 3 for a traffic incident involving a bicycle versus car collision.
Hi, I'm EMT Alex with the rescue squad. What happened to bring you here?
I was hurt in the accident. I have pain when I breathe.
Is the pain localized or reproducible on specific parts of your body?
It's on the right side of my chest and gets worse with deep breaths or touch there.
Before we proceed further, can you describe other areas of pain or discomfort?
I also have tenderness in my right upper and lower abdominal areas.
Can you stay awake and respond to questions right now?
Yes, I can.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15.
Blood pressure is 114 over 73.
Pulse is 90.
Respiratory rate is 20.
SpO2 is at 90.
EKG shows sinus rhythm.
We are placing a cervical collar to restrict motion and protect your spine.
I'm applying a 4-lead EKG for continuous cardiac monitoring.
Your airway is currently patent and not in distress.
Your breathing is normal in rate and depth.
Radial pulse is strong and regular.
Trachea is centered, no evidence of deviation is noted.
No visible signs of cyanosis, but there is mild pallor noted.
I see some abrasions and a hematoma across the right side of your face.
Trachea is centered and undeviated.
Bilateral breath sounds are present, but you described pain with deep breaths and palpation on the right.
You report tenderness in the right upper and lower abdominal quadrants.
We've placed an IV in your left arm with normal saline at a keep-vein-open rate.
A second IV has been placed in your right arm as a precautionary saline lock.
A 12-lead EKG was obtained. It shows normal sinus rhythm with an incomplete right bundle branch block.
Glucose is not currently measurable.
When did the pain first start after the accident?
Does specific movement or position make the pain worse?
How would you describe the quality of the chest pain? Sharp, or something else?
Does the chest pain travel or radiate anywhere else?
On a scale from 0 to 10, how severe is it now?
Has it been consistently 9 out of 10 since the incident today?
Repeat blood pressure is 103 over 69.
The 12-lead EKG continues to show normal sinus rhythm.
Respiratory rate is now 17 breaths per minute.
SpO2 improved to 94 percent.
I do not note any significant spinal pain with palpation, but there is some tenderness on your right flank.
I see abrasions to the knuckles of both hands.
JVD is not present.
No DCAPS noted on a basic scan of the body.
To better understand your condition, I need to know your medical history, medications, and any allergies.
I have no prior medical conditions, allergies, or medications.
When is the last time you had anything to drink or eat?
I had dinner around 5:30 PM.
Can you tell me what happened exactly at the time of the incident?
I was riding on my bicycle when a vehicle pulled out suddenly and hit me. I was thrown to the ground.
No drainage or obstruction from your nose observed.
No fluid draining from your ears and no signs of eardrum rupture.
No lymphadenopathy or neck masses are noted.
Work of breathing is not increased. You are breathing at your own pace with no signs of accessory muscle use.
We do not note any rectal bleeding or abnormal urine changes.
No signs of pelvic instability or dislocation reported.
I do not see any deformities or angulations in your hands or limbs.
Repeat airway evaluation finds it still clear and unobstructed.
Reportable respiratory rate increased again to 25 at one point but then stabilized.
Vital signs have remained within acceptable ranges throughout care.
Patient's condition remains stable during the transport without spikes or decline in vitals.
There have been no changes in location or intensity of the pain since assessment began.
Accessories such as IV and monitoring leads are all secured for transport.
ER team has been updated on arrival time and condition of the patient using the trauma alert protocol.
We remained unable to measure glucose values for the duration of your care.
I rechecked your airway and it remained patent and open while in repositioning.
Continued normal color and temperature of the skin has been noted with no jaundice or pallor perceived.
Reportable respiration quality showed no deviations from previous observations.
EKG status remains stable with normal sinus rhythm reported on all scans conducted.
Safety is ensured during transfer due to high attention on securing and immobilization from our team.
For your comfort, make sure to stay still and avoid sudden movements once transport begins.
No adjustments needed to your position during transport.
Communication has been sustained with dispatch for updates on your status en route.
Major signs remained stable including your pulse and respiration.
We maintained sinus rhythm visibility throughout with no major EKG incidents.
Skin turgor and temperature remain unchanged from our first evaluation.
Continued normal cardiac rhythm, with no new arrhythmias found.
Pupils remain equal and reactive, no dilation or slit changes.
I see no changes in mental status or behavior that would require additional attention.
There's been no notable changes in the amount of pain you are experiencing.
I rechecked your arm that received the initial IV. Flow remains stable without changes.
Providers at the receiving facility have been alerted and given a detailed summary of your condition.
Transport delay did not impact your condition as interventions were maintained at the scene and en route.
All transport checks have been passed, and we are heading to the hospital now at a high priority.
No new findings have been discovered since the last round of checks and reassessments.
Please keep communicating discomfort or stress if you feel anything change while we transport.
If you have any further issues in the ER, please inform the staff, who have been briefed about your situation.
We do not require aggressive fluid resuscitation for now based on your vital signs.
No signs of nuchal rigidity or abnormal extra-ocular movements were found.
Your skin generally appears intact and without rashes or bites.
We are transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] as a hot trauma alert. Trauma team has been alerted.
This is a high-priority transfer because you reported a brief loss of consciousness during the impact.
Patient care and report have now been turned over to ER staff without incident.
All emergent findings, including 12-lead EKG and IV access, have been fully reported.
According to protocol 4-1 for injury, we are following general trauma management guidelines today.
EKG has consistently remained in normal sinus rhythm since the first reading.
